1: A direct revelation: we have such a high priest, one who is seated at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in heaven, a minister in the holy places, in the true tent that the Lord set up, not man. The seated posture (contrast with the ever-standing Levitical priests, Hebrews 10:11) declares the completed work; the right-hand position declares the divine acceptance; the heavenly ministry declares the continuing access he secures. This is the summary of the entire priestly argument: a real high priest, with a real sanctuary, doing a real ministry — but heavenly rather than earthly, completed rather than ongoing, in the true tent rather than its copy.
6: A direct revelation: Christ has obtained a ministry that is as much more excellent than the old as the covenant he mediates is better, since it is enacted on better promises. The better ministry and better covenant are grounded in better promises — not merely better in terms of explicitness but better in terms of the reality they deliver: the internal law (not external command), the universal God-knowledge (not mediated instruction), the complete forgiveness (not annual atonement that remembers sins). All three improvements are grounded in Christ's completed sacrifice and Spirit-giving.
8: A fulfillment: "Behold, the days are coming, declares the Lord, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah" — the days that Jeremiah anticipated have come in Jesus. The new covenant that Jeremiah promised (internal law, complete forgiveness, universal God-knowledge) is the covenant that Jesus inaugurated with his blood (Luke 22:20: "this cup is the new covenant in my blood") and that his Spirit enacts in the community. The Jeremiah 31:31-34 citation is not merely predictive but structurally: it proves that the Mosaic covenant was designed to be temporary and that Christ's coming was God's planned fulfillment, not an improvisation.
2: A direct revelation: Christ serves in the sanctuary, the true tabernacle set up by the Lord, not by man. The contrast between the handmade (earthly) and the Lord-made (heavenly) sanctuaries defines where Christ's ministry operates: not in the shadow but in the reality, not in the copy but in the original. His ministry in the true tabernacle is the fulfillment of everything the earthly tabernacle anticipated — the location tells us the quality: this is the real thing, not the prototype.
3: A direct revelation: every high priest is appointed to offer gifts and sacrifices, so it was necessary for this one also to have something to offer. The necessity of Christ's offering establishes the continuity between his priesthood and the Levitical type: the structure (priest must offer) is the same; the content (what he offers) is different. He has something to offer — himself. The type required the priest to bring an animal; the antitype brings himself. The structure is fulfilled; the content is transformed.
4: A direct revelation: if he were on earth, he would not be a priest at all, since there are priests who offer gifts under the law. Christ's earthly non-priestly status (he was from Judah, not Levi; he performed no Levitical service) is itself a Christological marker: his priesthood operates in the heavenly sphere, not the earthly one. The earthly and heavenly are not competing claims but complementary ones — the earthly system was the shadow; the heavenly is the substance that Christ operates within.
5: A shadow: the Levitical priests serve a copy and shadow of the heavenly things — the Moses-pattern-on-the-mountain command establishes the entire earthly sanctuary as derivative from the heavenly original. The earthly tabernacle is the most sacred institution in Israel's history, and Hebrews calls it a "shadow" — not to devalue it but to reveal its nature. Shadows are real (they are cast by real objects) but they are not the objects themselves. The tabernacle was real revelation of the heavenly; Christ is the heavenly reality itself.
7: A direct revelation: if that first covenant had been faultless, no place would have been sought for another. The existence of Jeremiah's new covenant promise is the canonical proof that God himself acknowledged the Mosaic covenant's incompleteness. Christ's coming is therefore not a surprise or a course-correction but the arrival of what the first covenant was designed to herald. The first covenant was not a failure but a preparation; its inadequacy was by divine design, pointing forward to the covenant that Christ would inaugurate.
9: A fulfillment: "Not like the covenant I made with their fathers when I took them by the hand to lead them out of Egypt" — the Sinai covenant's contrast with the new covenant defines what the new covenant is not and therefore what it is. The old covenant was external (stone tablets), mediated (through Moses), and conditional (on Israel's faithfulness that they consistently failed to maintain). The new covenant that Christ inaugurates is internal (law on hearts), immediate (all will know God), and secured by God's own faithfulness rather than Israel's.
10: A fulfillment: "I will put my laws in their minds and write them on their hearts, and I will be their God, and they will be my people" — the internalized law of the new covenant is the Christ-given Spirit's work. The Spirit of Christ (Romans 8:9; 2 Corinthians 3:3) writes on the heart what the stone tablets could only inscribe externally. The covenant-formula ("I will be their God, they will be my people") reaches its full expression in the community that is united to Christ — the head of the people who are his body.
11: A fulfillment: "No longer will each one teach his neighbor or his brother, saying Know the Lord, for they shall all know me, from the least to the greatest" — the direct, universal God-knowledge of the new covenant is mediated through Christ the supreme teacher and revelation of the Father. The new covenant's universal God-knowledge is not the abolition of teaching but the transformation of its basis: not "learn about God" but "deepen your knowledge of the God you already know through Christ" (John 17:3: "this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent").
12: A fulfillment: "For I will be merciful toward their iniquities, and I will remember their sins no more" — the complete and permanent forgiveness of the new covenant is grounded in Christ's completed sacrifice. The promise's fulfillment is the specific consequence of the one offering that purified the conscience and secured eternal redemption (Hebrews 9:14; Hebrews 10:14). "I will remember their sins no more" is not a divine memory-failure but the covenant-completion that Christ's blood achieved: the record is permanently cleared.
13: A direct revelation: in speaking of a new covenant, God made the first one obsolete; what is growing old and aging will soon disappear. The obsolescence of the Mosaic covenant is not a judgment on its value but the declaration of its completed purpose: the shadow retires when the substance arrives. The Levitical system was always designed to be temporary — its annual cycles, its repeated sacrifices, its graduated access were all pointing forward to the once-for-all, fully open access that Christ secured. The "soon disappear" anticipates the Temple's destruction (70 CE) as the visible completion of what Christ's death and resurrection had already accomplished covenantally.
